name: hosted-agents-v2-py
description: Build hosted agents using Azure AI Projects SDK with ImageBasedHostedAgentDefinition. Use when creating container-based agents in Azure AI Foundry.
capabilities: Create container-based AI agents using ImageBasedHostedAgentDefinition
Configure CPU and memory resources for agent containers
Integrate tools like code interpreter and file search with agents
Manage agent lifecycle including creation, listing, and deletion
Set up environment variables for agent configuration
Implement both synchronous and asynchronous agent creation patterns
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis detected 79 potential issues across documentation code examples. After evaluation, all findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The file SKILL.md contains documentation with code examples showing shell commands (pip install, bash scripts) and environment variable access patterns typical for legitimate Azure AI development tutorials. No executable code is present. The 'weak cryptography' findings are false positives from detecting the word 'version' in context. No malicious intent or security risks identified.
valueStatement: Streamline container-based AI agent deployment on Azure using the official Projects SDK. Automate provisioning, scaling, and management of custom agent environments without manual infrastructure configuration.
userTitle: Deploy Azure AI Hosted Agents with Python
limitations: Requires Azure AI Foundry project and capability host configuration
Container images must be pre-built and pushed to Azure Container Registry
Network access required to Azure AI endpoints for SDK operations
Python SDK version 2.0.0b3 or higher is mandatory for hosted agent support
Resource allocation limited to maximum 4 CPU cores and 8GiB memory per agent

useCases.targetUser: Data engineers building automated ETL workflows
useCases.title: Data Processing Pipeline Agent
useCases.description: Deploy a containerized agent that processes large datasets using code interpreter tools, with automatic scaling based on workload demands.

useCases.title: Custom Tool Integration Agent
useCases.description: Create agents that connect to custom MCP servers and external APIs, enabling specialized business process automation within Azure AI infrastructure.

useCases.targetUser: DevOps teams managing multi-tenant AI platforms
useCases.title: Development Environment Agent
useCases.description: Provision isolated development environments with pre-configured tools and configurations for team collaboration and rapid prototyping.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Agent Creation
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a hosted agent using Azure AI Projects SDK. The agent should use container image 'myregistry.azurecr.io/my-agent:v1.0', require 2 CPU cores and 4GiB memory, support code interpreter tool, and set MODEL_NAME environment variable to 'gpt-4o-mini'.
promptTemplates.scenario: First-time deployment of a simple container-based agent

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Tool Agent Configuration
promptTemplates.prompt: Build an agent with both code interpreter and file search tools enabled. Connect to MCP server at 'https://tools.example.com' with label 'custom-tools'. Allocate 1 CPU core and 2GiB memory. Pass AZURE_AI_PROJECT_ENDPOINT from your environment to the agent.
promptTemplates.scenario: Advanced agent requiring multiple tool integrations

promptTemplates.title: Async Agent Deployment
promptTemplates.prompt: Implement an asynchronous agent creation workflow using AIProjectClient from azure.ai.projects.aio. Create an agent named 'async-processor' with minimal resources (0.5 CPU, 1GiB memory). Use async context managers for proper resource cleanup.
promptTemplates.scenario: High-throughput scenarios requiring concurrent agent operations

promptTemplates.title: Production-Ready Deployment
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a production agent deployment script with error handling. Include try/except blocks for ImagePullBackOff and InvalidContainerImage errors. Log agent state after creation. Implement cleanup logic to delete unused agent versions older than 30 days.
promptTemplates.scenario: Enterprise-grade deployment with monitoring and maintenance

bestPractices: Use specific image tags instead of 'latest' for reproducible deployments
Start with minimum resource allocation and scale up based on monitoring metrics
Store sensitive configuration in Azure Key Vault, reference via environment variables
Implement comprehensive error handling for common failures like ImagePullBackOff
Clean up unused agent versions regularly to optimize resource utilization
antiPatterns: Hardcoding secrets or API keys directly in environment_variables dictionary
Using 'latest' image tag in production deployments causes unpredictable behavior
Allocating maximum resources without testing actual usage patterns leads to waste
Skipping AcrPull role permissions setup causes authentication failures
Creating agent versions without deletion strategy accumulates unused resources

faq.question: What is the minimum SDK version required for hosted agents?
faq.answer: You must use azure-ai-projects version 2.0.0b3 or higher. Earlier versions do not support ImageBasedHostedAgentDefinition or the hosted agent APIs.

faq.question: How do I grant ACR pull permissions to my agent?
faq.answer: Navigate to your Azure Container Registry in the Azure portal, go to Access Control (IAM), and add the 'AcrPull' role for your AI project's managed identity.

faq.question: Can I use containers from Docker Hub or other registries?
faq.answer: No, hosted agents currently require images from Azure Container Registry (ACR). You must build and push your container image to ACR before creating the agent.

faq.question: What happens if my agent exceeds CPU or memory limits?
faq.answer: The agent will be throttled or terminated if it exceeds allocated resources. Monitor your agent's resource usage and adjust limits accordingly using the cpu and memory parameters.

faq.question: How do I debug ImagePullBackOff errors?
faq.answer: Verify the ACR pull permissions are granted, confirm the image path and tag exist in your registry, and ensure your ACR is accessible from the AI project's virtual network.

faq.question: Is synchronous or async client better for agent operations?
faq.answer: Use synchronous clients for simple scripts and one-time operations. Use async clients (azure.ai.projects.aio) for high-throughput scenarios requiring multiple concurrent agent operations.
